preparation_method: seasoned with soy sauce and oyster sauce

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of iron plate stir-fried cabbage with vermicelli is approximately 280 g, typically presented sizzling on a cast-iron plate.

nutrient_source.title: Stir-fried cabbage with glass noodles — composite dish estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: Cabbage, raw (FDC ID 169975); Mung bean noodles (glass noodles), cooked (FDC ID 168882); Soy sauce (FDC ID 172238); Vegetable oil (FDC ID 172336); Garlic, raw (FDC ID 169230)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for iron-plate stir-fried cabbage with vermicelli exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for the primary ingredients (cabbage ~55%, cooked glass noodles ~30%, oil ~5%, soy sauce ~5%, garlic and aromatics ~5%). Energy and macros are calculated from ingredient proportions. Sodium is elevated due to soy sauce and oyster sauce contributions. Values represent a plausible per-100g estimate for the cooked dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — glass noodles (mung bean vermicelli) GI approximately 33–39; cabbage GI approximately 10
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K et al. (2021) Diabetes Care
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for iron-plate stir-fried cabbage with vermicelli as a composite dish. GI is estimated at 35 based on the dominant carbohydrate contributor being mung bean glass noodles (GI ~33–39 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international tables). Cabbage contributes negligible glycemic load. The composite dish GI is anchored to glass noodles as the primary glycemic carbohydrate source. GL per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (35 × 32.2) / 100 ≈ 11.3, where available carbohydrate per 280 g serving = 11.5 × 2.8 = 32.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a standard 280 g serving provides approximately 1176 mg sodium, which represents over 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced-sauce preparation or limiting portion size is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index from mung bean glass noodles as primary carbohydrate source
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI of approximately 35, driven by mung bean vermicelli, makes this dish a relatively favourable carbohydrate choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, though portion control remains important as a standard serving delivers approximately 32 g of available carbohydrate.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high vegetable content, but moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is low in saturated fat (approximately 1.1 g per serving) and contains heart-protective cabbage rich in vitamin C and fibre; however, the sodium content from condiments warrants attention for individuals managing cardiovascular risk, and reduced-sodium soy sauce substitution is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Iron Plate Stir-Fried Cabbage with Vermicelli (铁板粉丝炒包菜, Tiě bǎn fěnsī chǎo bāocài) is a popular Chinese stir-fried dish served sizzling on a preheated cast-iron plate, which imparts a distinctive smoky, caramelised aroma to the ingredients. The dish features shredded round cabbage (bao cai) and mung bean glass noodles (fensi) as its primary components, stir-fried together with garlic, soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sesame oil over high heat. The iron plate cooking method creates a slight char on the cabbage edges and noodles, producing a complex flavour profile that combines savoury umami notes from the soy and oyster sauces with the natural sweetness of the cabbage. The texture contrasts soft, translucent glass noodles with tender-crisp cabbage strands. This dish is widely served in Chinese restaurants and zi char (煮炒) stalls across Singapore and Malaysia. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy dish at approximately 82 kcal per 100 g, with a low glycemic index of approximately 35 due to the mung bean vermicelli, making it a relatively suitable carbohydrate choice for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is low in saturated fat but contains significant sodium from the condiments used, which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It provides modest amounts of vitamin C and dietary fibre from the cabbage. The dish is dairy-free and suitable for those avoiding dairy, but contains soy and shellfish allergens from the sauces.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this composite dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional food composition database. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy ingredient entries. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for mung bean glass noodles as the dominant glycemic carbohydrate. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient composition is well-defined and the USDA SR Legacy sources are high quality; however, the composite estimation introduces uncertainty.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA SR Legacy ingredient entries; no exact dish-level entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this dish uses vegetable oil and plant-based ingredients with no industrial trans fat sources. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as all primary ingredients are plant-based (cabbage, glass noodles, vegetable oil, garlic, soy sauce). 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients contributing B12. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as no vitamin D-containing ingredients are present. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g (1.8), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.8), potassium_mg (148.0), calcium_mg (32.0), iron_mg (0.5), vitamin_c_mg (18.0), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (5.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.04), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.03), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (0.4), vitamin_b6_mg (0.09), folate_mcg_dfe (22.0), vitamin_e_mg (0.6), phosphorus_mg (38.0), magnesium_mg (12.0), zinc_mg (0.2), selenium_mcg (0.8), water_g (80.5) are all estimated from USDA SR Legacy ingredient proportions. 7. Halal status is marked false as oyster sauce (shellfish-derived) is a standard ingredient; halal-certified versions may exist but cannot be assumed. 8. The dish is served at See Ya Tonight (今晚有局) restaurant in Singapore at SGD 15.34. 9. GI set to 35 as an analogous estimate anchored to mung bean glass noodles per University of Sydney GI Database; gi_category is low.
